questions.stem: The free group $F(S)$ on a set $S$ is best described as the group whose elements are:
options: the abelian group generated by $S$ with no relations.
reduced words in the symbols of $S$ and their formal inverses, with multiplication by concatenation followed by reduction.
questions.explanation: The free group on $S$ consists of reduced words $s_1^{\pm 1} \cdots s_k^{\pm 1}$ in the generators, where reduced means no adjacent pair $s s^{-1}$ or $s^{-1} s$ appears. The product of two words is their concatenation, followed by cancelling any such adjacent inverse pairs.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: A word in the generators of a free group and their inverses is called reduced when:
options: it contains no two adjacent symbols of the form $s s^{-1}$ or $s^{-1} s$.
every generator appears at most once.
questions.explanation: A reduced word is one in which no generator stands immediately next to its own inverse, so no further cancellation is possible. Each element of the free group is represented by exactly one reduced word, which is what makes the normal form unique.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The universal property of the free group $F(S)$ states that any function $f : S \to G$ into a group $G$:
options: extends to a unique group homomorphism $\bar{f} : F(S) \to G$.
extends to a unique homomorphism only when $G$ is abelian.
questions.explanation: The free group is the free object on $S$ in the category of groups: every set map $f : S \to G$ factors uniquely through the inclusion $S \hookrightarrow F(S)$ as a homomorphism $\bar{f}$. This universal property characterizes $F(S)$ up to canonical isomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The free group on a single generator is isomorphic to:
questions.explanation: With one generator $a$, the reduced words are exactly the powers $a^n$ for $n \in \mathbb{Z}$, and concatenation adds exponents. Hence $F(\{a\}) \cong \mathbb{Z}$, the infinite cyclic group.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The free group $F_2$ on two generators $a, b$ is:
options: nonabelian, since $ab$ and $ba$ are distinct reduced words.
questions.explanation: In $F_2$ the words $ab$ and $ba$ are both reduced and not equal, so $ab \ne ba$ and the group is nonabelian. This contrasts with the free abelian group $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, in which the generators commute by definition.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: A group presentation $\langle S \mid R \rangle$ denotes:
options: the quotient of the free group $F(S)$ by the normal subgroup generated by the relators $R$.
the free group $F(S)$ together with the set $R$ as a subgroup.
questions.explanation: The presentation $\langle S \mid R \rangle$ is $F(S) / N$, where $N$ is the smallest normal subgroup of the free group $F(S)$ containing the relators in $R$. Setting the relators equal to the identity imposes exactly the relations $R$ on the generators $S$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}/n$ has the presentation:
questions.explanation: The presentation $\langle a \mid a^n \rangle$ takes the infinite cyclic group on $a$ and imposes $a^n = 1$, giving a cyclic group of order $n$. So $\mathbb{Z}/n \cong \langle a \mid a^n \rangle$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Which presentation describes the free abelian group $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$?
questions.explanation: The single relator $aba^{-1}b^{-1}$ is the commutator $[a,b]$; setting it to $1$ forces $ab = ba$. Thus $\langle a, b \mid aba^{-1}b^{-1} \rangle \cong \mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$, the free abelian group of rank $2$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The dihedral group $D_n$ of order $2n$ admits the presentation:
options: $\langle r, s \mid r^n, s^2, rs = sr \rangle$.
questions.explanation: The dihedral group is generated by a rotation $r$ with $r^n = 1$ and a reflection $s$ with $s^2 = 1$, related by $srs^{-1} = r^{-1}$, i.e. $srs^{-1}r = 1$. The option with $rs = sr$ would force the group to be abelian, which $D_n$ is not for $n \ge 3$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The free product $G \ast H$ of two groups is the group whose elements are:
options: ordered pairs $(g, h)$ with componentwise multiplication.
reduced words that alternate between nontrivial elements of $G$ and nontrivial elements of $H$.
the elements of $G$ together with the elements of $H$ and nothing more.
questions.explanation: Each nontrivial element of $G \ast H$ is represented uniquely by a reduced word $x_1 x_2 \cdots x_k$ in which consecutive letters come from different factors and no letter is the identity. Multiplication is concatenation followed by combining or cancelling adjacent letters from the same factor; this is the normal form for the free product.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The free product $\mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z}$ is isomorphic to:
options: the free group $F_2$ on two generators.
questions.explanation: Taking a generator from each copy of $\mathbb{Z}$ gives two free generators with no relations between them, so $\mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \cong F_2$. Note that the free product is nonabelian, unlike the direct product $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The free product $G \ast H$ is characterized by which universal property?
options: It is the coproduct of $G$ and $H$ in the category of groups.
It is the product of $G$ and $H$ in the category of groups.
It is the largest abelian quotient of $G \times H$.
It is the kernel of the map $G \times H \to G$.
questions.explanation: The free product is the coproduct in the category of groups: given homomorphisms $G \to K$ and $H \to K$, there is a unique homomorphism $G \ast H \to K$ restricting to them on each factor. This universal mapping property determines $G \ast H$ up to isomorphism.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: The free product with amalgamation $G \ast_K H$ is formed from $G \ast H$ by:
options: deleting the subgroup $K$ from both factors.
imposing the relations that identify the two images of each element of $K$, one coming from $G$ and one from $H$.
taking the direct product of $G$ and $H$ over $K$.
questions.explanation: Given homomorphisms $K \to G$ and $K \to H$, the amalgamated product $G \ast_K H$ is the quotient of $G \ast H$ by the normal subgroup generated by the elements $i(k)\, j(k)^{-1}$ for $k \in K$. This glues the two copies of $K$ together inside the free product.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: In category-theoretic terms, the amalgamated free product $G \ast_K H$ is:
options: the pullback of $G \to K$ and $H \to K$.
the equalizer of two maps $K \to G \times H$.
the pushout of the two homomorphisms $K \to G$ and $K \to H$ in the category of groups.
questions.explanation: The amalgamated product is exactly the pushout of the diagram $G \leftarrow K \rightarrow H$ in the category of groups. This is the algebraic structure that the Seifert-Van Kampen theorem identifies as the fundamental group of a union.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: In the Seifert-Van Kampen theorem, the space $X$ is written as a union $X = U \cup V$ where $U$ and $V$ are required to be:
questions.explanation: The standard hypothesis is that $U$ and $V$ are open sets whose union is $X$. Openness is what allows loops and homotopies to be subdivided so that each piece lies in $U$ or in $V$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Besides $U$ and $V$ being open with $X = U \cup V$, the Seifert-Van Kampen theorem requires that:
options: $U$, $V$, and $U \cap V$ are all path-connected and contain the basepoint $x_0$.
questions.explanation: The hypotheses are that $U$, $V$, and the intersection $U \cap V$ are each path-connected and all contain the chosen basepoint $x_0$. The path-connectedness of $U \cap V$ is essential; without it the simple form of the theorem can fail.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Under its hypotheses, the Seifert-Van Kampen theorem identifies $\pi_1(X, x_0)$ with:
options: the free product $\pi_1(U) \ast \pi_1(V)$ amalgamated over $\pi_1(U \cap V)$.
questions.explanation: The theorem states $\pi_1(X) \cong \pi_1(U) \ast_{\pi_1(U \cap V)} \pi_1(V)$, the pushout of the maps induced by the inclusions of $U \cap V$ into $U$ and into $V$. The amalgamation over $\pi_1(U \cap V)$ records the relations coming from loops in the overlap.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: If, in addition to the standard hypotheses, the intersection $U \cap V$ is simply connected, then the theorem gives $\pi_1(X) \cong$:
questions.explanation: When $\pi_1(U \cap V)$ is trivial there are no relations to amalgamate, so the amalgamated product collapses to the ordinary free product $\pi_1(U) \ast \pi_1(V)$. This is the most commonly used special case of the theorem.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Modelling the figure eight as $U \cup V$, with each of $U$ and $V$ deformation retracting to one circle and $U \cap V$ contractible, the theorem yields $\pi_1(S^1 \vee S^1) \cong$:
options: $\mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \cong F_2$, the free group on two generators.
questions.explanation: Each open set has fundamental group $\mathbb{Z}$ and the overlap is simply connected, so the theorem gives the free product $\mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z} \cong F_2$. The wedge of two circles therefore has free fundamental group on two generators.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: More generally, applying the theorem to a wedge of $n$ circles gives a fundamental group isomorphic to:
questions.explanation: Iterating the free-product computation over the $n$ circles, each contributing one generator and the overlaps remaining simply connected, gives $\pi_1(\bigvee_n S^1) \cong F_n$, the free group of rank $n$. For $n \ge 2$ this group is nonabelian.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: To compute $\pi_1(S^n)$ for $n \ge 2$ via Seifert-Van Kampen, one covers $S^n$ by two open sets $U$ and $V$ chosen as:
options: a single point and its complement.
slightly enlarged open caps about the north and south poles, each contractible, with $U \cap V$ path-connected.
questions.explanation: Take $U$ and $V$ to be the sphere minus the south pole and minus the north pole; each is homeomorphic to $\mathbb{R}^n$ and hence contractible. For $n \ge 2$ the overlap $U \cap V$ deformation retracts onto $S^{n-1}$, which is path-connected, so the hypotheses hold.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Carrying out that computation, the fundamental group of $S^n$ for $n \ge 2$ is:
options: the trivial group, so $S^n$ is simply connected.
questions.explanation: Both $U$ and $V$ are contractible, so $\pi_1(U)$ and $\pi_1(V)$ are trivial, and the free product of trivial groups is trivial. Hence $\pi_1(S^n)$ is trivial for $n \ge 2$; in particular $\pi_1(S^2)$ is trivial and $S^2$ is simply connected.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Why does the two-cap argument for $\pi_1(S^n)$ break down when $n = 1$?
options: Because $S^1$ is not a topological space.
Because the open caps fail to be contractible.
Because for $n = 1$ the overlap $U \cap V$ is not path-connected, so a key hypothesis fails.
Because $S^1$ cannot be covered by two open sets.
questions.explanation: For $n = 1$, removing two antipodal points leaves an overlap homeomorphic to two disjoint arcs, which is not path-connected, so the path-connectedness hypothesis on $U \cap V$ fails. This is exactly why $\pi_1(S^1) \cong \mathbb{Z}$ is nontrivial rather than trivial, and the naive free-product conclusion does not apply.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Presenting the torus $T^2$ as a square with edges identified and a single $2$-cell attached along $aba^{-1}b^{-1}$, the Seifert-Van Kampen theorem gives $\pi_1(T^2) \cong$:
questions.explanation: The $1$-skeleton is a wedge of two circles with free group $\langle a, b \rangle$, and attaching the $2$-cell along the boundary word $aba^{-1}b^{-1}$ imposes the single relation $aba^{-1}b^{-1} = 1$. The resulting presentation $\langle a, b \mid aba^{-1}b^{-1} \rangle$ is the free abelian group $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Recall the basic free groups and presentations.
questions.solution: The free group on a single generator is isomorphic to {{1}}. The presentation $\langle a \mid a^n \rangle$ describes the cyclic group of order {{2}}. The free group $F_2$ on two generators {{3}} abelian.
questions.explanation: The free group on one generator is the infinite cyclic group $\mathbb{Z}$, and $\langle a \mid a^n \rangle$ has order $n$. Since $ab \ne ba$ in $F_2$, the free group on two generators is not abelian.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: State the hypotheses of the Seifert-Van Kampen theorem for $X = U \cup V$.
questions.solution: The sets $U$ and $V$ must be {{1}} subsets whose union is $X$. The number of the three sets $U$, $V$, $U \cap V$ that are required to be path-connected and to contain the basepoint is {{2}}. When the intersection $U \cap V$ is simply connected, the conclusion simplifies to the plain {{3}} of $\pi_1(U)$ and $\pi_1(V)$.
questions.explanation: The theorem requires open sets $U$ and $V$, with all three of $U$, $V$, and $U \cap V$ path-connected and sharing the basepoint, so the count is $3$. If $U \cap V$ is simply connected the amalgamation is trivial and $\pi_1(X)$ is the free product $\pi_1(U) \ast \pi_1(V)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Apply the theorem to wedges of circles.
questions.solution: The wedge of two circles has fundamental group the free group $F_2$, equal to the free product $\mathbb{Z} \ast \mathbb{Z}$. A wedge of five circles has fundamental group the free group of rank {{1}}. As an amalgamated product over $\pi_1$ of the contractible overlap, this computation realizes $\pi_1(X)$ as a {{2}} in the category of groups.
questions.explanation: A wedge of $n$ circles has fundamental group $F_n$, so for five circles the rank is $5$. The Seifert-Van Kampen theorem expresses $\pi_1(X)$ as the pushout of $\pi_1(U) \leftarrow \pi_1(U \cap V) \rightarrow \pi_1(V)$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)

questions.stem: Compute fundamental groups of the sphere and the torus.
questions.solution: Covering $S^2$ by two contractible caps with path-connected overlap, the free product of two trivial groups shows $\pi_1(S^2)$ is the {{1}} group. Attaching a $2$-cell to the wedge of two circles along the word $aba^{-1}b^{-1}$, the torus has $\pi_1(T^2) \cong$ {{2}}. The number of generators in the standard presentation of $\pi_1(T^2)$ is {{3}}.
questions.explanation: Two contractible caps give a free product of trivial groups, so $\pi_1(S^2)$ is trivial. The torus presentation $\langle a, b \mid aba^{-1}b^{-1} \rangle$ has $2$ generators and yields $\mathbb{Z} \times \mathbb{Z}$.
questions.source: in the style of a KNUST Algebraic Topology I course (MATH 479)
